en.extract:
The yellow-eyed penguin (Megadyptes antipodes), known also as hoiho, is a species of penguin endemic to New Zealand. It is the sole extant species in the genus Megadyptes, from Ancient Greek μέγας (mégas), meaning "large", and δύπτης (dúptes), meaning "diver".
Previously thought closely related to the little penguin (Eudyptula minor), molecular research has shown it more closely related to penguins of the genus Eudyptes. Like most penguins, it is mainly piscivorous.
The species breeds along the eastern and south-eastern coastlines of the South Island of New Zealand, as well as Stewart Island, Auckland Islands, and Campbell Islands. Colonies on the Otago Peninsula are a popular tourist venue, where visitors may closely observe penguins from hides, trenches, or tunnels.
On the New Zealand mainland, the species has experienced a significant decline over the past 20 years. On the Otago Peninsula, numbers have dropped by 75% since the mid-1990s and population trends indicate the possibility of extirpation from the peninsula in the next 20 to 40 years. While the effect of rising ocean temperatures is still being studied, an infectious outbreak in the mid-2000s played a large role in the drop. Human activities at sea (fisheries, pollution) may have an equal if not greater influence on the species' downward trend.


Taxonomy
The yellow-eyed penguin was first described by Jacques Bernard Hombron and Honoré Jacquinot in 1841.
The yellow-eyed penguin is the sole species in the genus Megadyptes, though there have been multiple subspecies. It was previously thought closely related to the little penguin, but molecular research has shown it is more closely related to penguins of the genus Eudyptes. Mitochondrial and nuclear DNA evidence suggests it split from the ancestors of Eudyptes around 15 million years ago. In 2019 the 1.25Gb genome of the species was published as part of the Penguin Genome Consortium, aiming to understand the origins and aid conservation by helping to inform any future breeding programmes.


Subspecies


Megadyptes antipodes antipodes
The hoiho or yellow-eyed penguin (M. a. antipodes) is the only extant subspecies. They were formerly most abundant in the subantarctic Auckland and Campbell Islands, and colonised Stewart Island / Rakiura and parts of the South Island following the extinction of the Waitaha penguin. 
There are proposals to split the yellow-eyed penguin into three further subspecies due to genetic differences. Genome sequencing of 249 penguins from across the New Zealand mainland and the subantarctic Enderby and Campbell Islands identified clear genetic divisions in each location with negligible gene flow between the populations, consistent with there being three distinct subspecies. Comparison of these genomes with DNA from two extinct subspecies showed that the three lineages diverged between 5,000 and 16,000 years ago.


Megadyptes antipodes waitaha
The Waitaha penguin (M. a. waitaha) is an extinct subspecies that was present in the North Island, the South Island, Stewart Island, and Codfish Island / Whenua Hou. Last dated to AD 1347–1529. It was discovered by University of Otago and University of Adelaide scientists comparing the foot bones of 500-year-old, 100-year-old and modern specimens of penguins. According to lead researcher Sanne Boessenkool, Waitaha penguins "were around 10% smaller than the yellow-eyed penguin. The two species are very closely related, but we can't say if they had a yellow crown." The penguin was named for the Māori iwi (tribe) Waitaha, whose tribal lands included the areas the Waitaha penguin are thought to have inhabited. "Our findings demonstrate that yellow-eyed penguins on mainland New Zealand are not a declining remnant of a previous abundant population, but came from the subantarctic relatively recently and replaced the extinct Waitaha penguin," said team member Dr Jeremy Austin, deputy director of the Australasian Centre for Ancient DNA. Archaeological remains indicate that early Polynesian settlers hunted the subspecies and that this, with possible additional predation by Polynesian rats and dogs, was a probable cause of extinction. As the local Māori people have no record of this subspecies, it is estimated to have perished between c. 1300 and 1500, soon after Polynesian settlers arrived in New Zealand. It was first described as a new species M. waitaha in 2009, but reclassified as a subspecies M. a. waitaha in studies from 2019 and 2022. After their extinction, their range was occupied by yellow-eyed penguins (now M. a. antipodes), previously most abundant further south in the subantarctic islands. The decrease in sea lion populations after human settlement may also have eased their expansion. Another coauthor, Dr Phil Seddon, said "these unexpected results highlight ... the dynamic nature of ecosystem change, where the loss of one species may open up opportunities for the expansion of another."


Megadyptes antipodes richdalei
Richdale's penguin (M. a. richdalei) is an extinct island dwarf subspecies from the Chatham Islands.  Last dated after the 13th century, it was hunted to extinction.


Description

The yellow-eyed penguin (M. a. antipodes) is most easily identified by the band of pale yellow feathers surrounding its eyes and encircling the back of its head. Its forehead, crown and the sides of its face are slate grey flecked with golden yellow. Its eye is yellow. The foreneck and sides of the head are light brown. The back and tail are slate blue-black. Its chest, stomach, thighs and the underside of its flippers are white in colour. Juvenile birds have a greyer head with no yellow band around their eyes.
It is the largest living penguin to breed on the mainland of New Zealand and the fourth or fifth heaviest living penguin by body mass. It stands 62–79 centimetres (24–31 in) tall and weighs  3–8.5 kilograms (6.6–18.7 lb). Weight varies throughout the year, with penguins being heaviest just before moulting, during which they may lose 3–4 kilograms in weight. Males at around 5.53 kg (12.2 lb) on average are somewhat heavier than females at an average of 5.13 kg (11.3 lb).
The yellow-eyed penguin may be long lived, with some individuals reaching 20 years of age. Males are generally longer lived than females, leading to a sex ratio of 2:1 around the age of 10–12 years.
The yellow-eyed penguin is mostly silent. It makes a shrill bray-like call at nest and breeding sites.


Distribution and habitat

Until recently, it was assumed that M. a. antipodes was widespread and abundant before the arrival of Polynesian settlers in New Zealand. However, genetic analysis has since revealed that its range only expanded to include mainland New Zealand in the past 200 years. Yellow-eyed penguins expanded out of the subantarctic to replace New Zealand's endemic Waitaha penguin (M. a. waitaha). The Waitaha penguin became extinct between about 1300 and 1500, soon after Polynesian settlers arrived in New Zealand.  Jeremy Austin, a member of the team that discovered the Waitaha penguin, said, "Our findings demonstrate that yellow-eyed penguins on mainland New Zealand are not a declining remnant of a previous abundant population, but came from the subantarctic relatively recently and replaced the extinct Waitaha penguin."
An island dwarf subspecies from the Chatham Islands, M. a. richdalei, is extinct. The modern population of yellow-eyed penguins does not breed on the Chatham Islands.
Today, yellow-eyed penguins are found in two distinct populations. The northern population extends along the southeast coast of the South Island of New Zealand, down to Stewart Island / Rakiura and Codfish Island / Whenua Hou. It includes four main breeding areas in Banks Peninsula, North Otago, Otago Peninsula and the Catlins. It is also referred to as the mainland population. The southern population includes the subantarctic Auckland Islands and Campbell Island / Motu Ihupuku. There is little gene flow between the northern and southern populations as the large stretch of ocean between the South Island and subantarctic region and the subtropical convergence act as a natural barrier. Based on monitoring between 2012-2017, there are on average 577 breeding pairs per year on Enderby Island in the Auckland Islands, which comprise 37-49% of the total breeding population for the species.


Behaviour


Breeding

The nesting behaviour of yellow-eyed penguins is thought to be the least colonial of all penguin species. While other penguin species nest in large aggregations, yellow-eyed penguins tend to nest in solitary pairs or loose aggregations.  
While they can be seen coming ashore in groups of four to six or more individuals, they then disperse along tracks to individual nest sites up to one kilometre inland. Accordingly, the consensus among New Zealand penguin workers is to use habitat rather than colony to refer to areas where yellow-eyed penguins nest.
The species prefers to nest in forest or scrub habitat with dense vegetation cover. Adults will incorporate vegetation such as twigs, grasses and leaves into their nests, and often return to the same nesting area each year. Breeding sites are frequented year-round, and adults may continue to defend their nest areas outside of the breeding season. Nests tend to be visually isolated from one another, though they are usually in audible distance from each other.
Yellow-eyed penguins begin forming pairs and breeding at around two years of age. Nest site selection takes place from June onwards, with breeding beginning in late August, followed by incubation from September to November.
After hatching chicks are guarded by an adult full-time for the first 40–50 days. Incubation duties are shared by both parents, who may spend several days on the nest at a time. For the first six weeks after hatching, the chicks are guarded during the day by one parent while the other is at sea feeding. The foraging adult returns at least daily to feed the chicks and relieve the partner. After the chicks are six weeks of age, both parents go to sea to supply food to their rapidly growing offspring. Chicks usually fledge in mid-February and are totally independent from then on. Chick fledge weights are generally between 5 and 6 kg.


Feeding

Around 90% of the yellow-eyed penguin's diet is made up of fish, chiefly demersal species that live near the seafloor, including silversides (Argentina elongata), blue cod (Parapercis colias), red cod (Pseudophycis bachus), and opalfish (Hemerocoetes monopterygius). Other species taken are New Zealand blueback sprat (Sprattus antipodum) and cephalopods such as arrow squid (Nototodarus sloanii). They also eat some crustaceans, including krill (Nyctiphanes australis). Recently, jellyfish were found to be targeted by the penguins. While initially thought that the birds would prey on jellyfish itself, deployments of camera loggers revealed that the penguins were going after juvenile fish and fish larvae associated with jellyfish.
Breeding penguins usually undertake two kinds of foraging trips: day trips where the birds leave at dawn and return in the evening ranging up to 25 km from their colonies, and shorter evening trips during which the birds are seldom away from their nest longer than four hours or range farther than 7 km. Yellow-eyed penguins are known to be an almost exclusive benthic forager that searches for prey along the seafloor. Accordingly, up to 90% of their dives are benthic dives. This also means that their average dive depths are determined by the water depths within their home ranges, but can swim up to 240 meters below the water surface.


Conservation

The yellow-eyed penguin is considered one of the rarest penguin species in the world. It is listed on the IUCN Red List as being endangered. It was first included on the list in 1988 when it was listed as threatened, and was changed to endangered in the year 2000. 


Northern population
Around a third of the total yellow-eyed penguin population live on the mainland. Data from the Department of Conservation showed that the northern population had collapsed by 80 percent since 2008, from 739 breeding pairs to just 143 in 2025. Experts is likely that the group could disappear within the next 20 years if the trend continues. The last breeding-age female at Sandfly Bay died in 2024, after being caught in a set net, leaving the site functionally extinct.


Southern population
The subantarctic population, where the majority of the species breeds, is less studied. Monitoring of the 2015 to 2017 breeding seasons found that fledging success tended to be higher than on the mainland, although chicks were smaller and lighter on average. 


Threats

Populations of yellow-eyed penguins are threatened by multiple factors. 
A risk assessment published in November 2025 indicated the biggest threats to the species are malnutrition, predation, disease, other trauma/unidentifiable sources, and fishing. 
The northern population is declining as a result of recurring poor breeding seasons and ongoing high adult mortality. Threats at sea include poor foraging success, and becoming caught in fisheries equipment. Chicks are often lost at sea, with only 20% projected to make it back to land for nesting, and only 5% expected to breed.
Studies carried out during the 1980s showed that red cod is a key part of their diet, however red cod populations have since declined, and more recent investigation has found that the penguins are feeding on blue cod instead, which provide less suitable nutrition.  
Disease also poses a key threat to the species, which poor nutrition makes them more vulnerable to. A previously undescribed disease killed off 60% of yellow-eyed penguin chicks on the Otago Peninsula and in North Otago in 2004. The disease has been described as diphtheritic stomatitis, caused by  infection of Corynebacterium, a genus of bacteria that also causes diphtheria in humans. A similar problem has affected the Stewart Island population.
In 2023, a novel Gyrovirus was identified as the likely cause of the mysterious and fatal respiratory illness which had killed roughly a quarter of the yellow-eyed penguin chicks from the mainland during the 2021 November to December hatching season. The disease has a mortality rate of more than 90%. It was estimated in 2023 around 320 individuals died.


Mitigations
A reserve protecting more than 10% of the mainland population was established at Long Point in the Catlins in November 2007 by the Department of Conservation and the Yellow-eyed Penguin Trust. The species was granted protection under the U.S. Endangered Species Act in 2010.
Due to extreme disease outbreak and subsequent mortalities, the Dunedin Wildlife Hospital began treating chicks with disease symptoms or weight loss. In 2022, the hospital successfully incubated and hatched eggs for the first time. The following year, the hospital hand-reared 214 chicks, as it was estimated that 50 to 70% of chicks would have died without intervention.
In September 2025, a three-month emergency ban on set net fishing was put in place around Otago Peninsula to protect the yellow-eyed penguin. Later that year the ban was extended by another nine months. The closure extended the existing four nautical mile set net ban, which was in place to protect dolphins, out to eight nautical miles. The ban came after urgent calls from conservation groups as the species entered their breeding season. The following month, the Environmental Law Initiative (ELI) filed High Court proceedings against the Minister for Oceans and Fisheries on the basis that the set net ban failed to protect the species against extinction. The extension applied to waters surrounding the Otago Peninsula, leaving habitats including North Otago and Stewart Island unprotected. In February 2026, the proceedings filed by ELI against the Minister were dismissed by the High Court. In the same ruling, the court recognised that the temporary emergency closure is lawful, and that ministers must work to safeguard the species’ survival.   


Tourism
Several mainland habitats have hides and are relatively accessible for those wishing to watch the birds come ashore. These include beaches at Oamaru, the Moeraki lighthouse, a number of beaches near Dunedin, and the Catlins.  In addition, commercial tourist operations on Otago Peninsula also provide hides to view yellow-eyed penguins. However, the yellow-eyed penguin cannot be found in zoos because it will not reproduce in captivity. Studies have shown however, that human presence in their habitats negatively impacts their foraging and breeding habits.


In culture
The hoiho appears on the reverse side of the New Zealand five-dollar note.
The yellow-eyed penguin is the mascot to Dunedin City Council's recycling and solid waste management campaign.
The yellow-eyed penguin is also featured in Farce of the Penguins, in which they complain about global warming.
In 2019 the yellow-eyed penguin was crowned the Bird of the Year in New Zealand, the first win for a seabird in the competition's 14-year history. It was again victorious in the 2024 competition.

As the local [[Māori people]] have no record of this subspecies, it is estimated to have perished between c. 1300 and 1500, soon after [[Immigration to New Zealand|Polynesian settlers arrived in New Zealand]].{{cite news |first=Rebecca |last=Fox |title=Ancient species of penguin found in DNA of bones |url=http://www.odt.co.nz/on-campus/university-otago/32502/ancient-species-penguin-found-dna-bones |newspaper=[[Otago Daily Times]] |date=2008-11-20 |access-date=2008-11-20 |archive-date=9 June 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110609001743/http://www.odt.co.nz/on-campus/university-otago/32502/ancient-species-penguin-found-dna-bones }} It was first described as a new species ''M. waitaha'' in 2009,{{Cite journal |last1=Boessenkool |first1=Sanne |last2=Austin |first2=Jeremy J |last3=Worthy |first3=Trevor H |last4=Scofield |first4=Paul |last5=Cooper |first5=Alan |last6=Seddon |first6=Philip J |last7=Waters |first7=Jonathan M |date=2009-03-07 |title=Relict or colonizer? Extinction and range expansion of penguins in southern New Zealand |journal=Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences |volume=276 |issue=1658 |pages=815–821 |doi=10.1098/rspb.2008.1246 |issn=0962-8452 |pmc=2664357 |pmid=19019791}} but reclassified as a subspecies ''M. a. waitaha'' in studies from 2019{{cite journal |last1=Cole |first1=T.L. |last2=Ksepka |first2=D.T. |last3=Mitchell |first3=K.J. |last4=Tennyson |first4=A.J. |last5=Thomas |first5=D.B. |last6=Pan |first6=H. |last7=Zhang |first7=G. |last8=Rawlence |first8=N.J. |last9=Wood |first9=J.R. |last10=Bover |first10=P. |last11=Bouzat |first11=J.L. |year=2019 |title=Mitogenomes uncover extinct penguin taxa and reveal island formation as a key driver of speciation |journal=Molecular Biology and Evolution |volume=36 |issue=4 |pages=784–797 |doi=10.1093/molbev/msz017 |pmid=30722030|doi-access=free }} and 2022.{{Cite journal |last1=Cole |first1=Theresa L. |last2=Zhou |first2=Chengran |last3=Fang |first3=Miaoquan |last4=Pan |first4=Hailin |last5=Ksepka |first5=Daniel T. |last6=Fiddaman |first6=Steven R. |last7=Emerling |first7=Christopher A. |last8=Thomas |first8=Daniel B. |last9=Bi |first9=Xupeng |last10=Fang |first10=Qi |last11=Ellegaard |first11=Martin R. |last12=Feng |first12=Shaohong |last13=Smith |first13=Adrian L. |last14=Heath |first14=Tracy A. |last15=Tennyson |first15=Alan J. D. |date=2022-07-19 |title=Genomic insights into the secondary aquatic transition of penguins |journal=Nature Communications |language=en |volume=13 |issue=1 |page=3912 |doi=10.1038/s41467-022-31508-9 |issn=2041-1723|doi-access=free |pmid=35853876 |pmc=9296559 |bibcode=2022NatCo..13.3912C |hdl=11250/3049871 |hdl-access=free }} After their extinction, their range was occupied by yellow-eyed penguins (now ''M. a. antipodes''), previously most abundant further south in [[New Zealand Subantarctic Islands|the subantarctic islands]]. The decrease in sea lion populations after human settlement may also have eased their expansion. Another coauthor, Dr Phil Seddon, said "these unexpected results highlight ... the dynamic nature of ecosystem change, where the loss of one species may open up opportunities for the expansion of another."{{cite press release| title = Penguin bones reveal long-lost species| publisher = Science Media Centre| date = 19 November 2008| url = http://www.sciencemediacentre.co.nz/2008/11/19/penguin-bones-reveal-long-lost-species/| access-date = 2 December 2008}} ==== ''Megadyptes antipodes richdalei'' ==== Richdale's penguin (''M. a. richdalei'') is an extinct [[Insular dwarfism|island dwarf]] subspecies from the [[Chatham Islands]]. Last dated after the 13th century, it was hunted to extinction. == Description == [[File:Molting yellow-eyed penguin IMG 6073.jpg|upright|left|thumb|[[Molt]]ing yellow-eyed penguin at [[Oamaru]], New Zealand]] The yellow-eyed penguin (''M. a. antipodes'') is most easily identified by the band of pale yellow feathers surrounding its eyes and encircling the back of its head.{{Cite book|title=The Field Guide to the Birds of New Zealand |last1=Heather |first1=Barrie D. |last2=Robertson |first2=Hugh A. |date = 2015 |others=Onley, Derek J. |publisher=Penguin Random House|edition=Revised and updated |location=[Auckland], New Zealand |page=40 |isbn=978-0-14-357092-9 |oclc=946520191}} Its forehead, crown and the sides of its face are slate grey flecked with golden yellow.{{Cite web |url=https://www.penguins.org.nz/yellow-eyed-penguin-biology.html |title=Yellow-eyed penguin biology |website=Penguin Rescue|access-date=2019-09-29|archive-date=29 September 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190929012956/https://www.penguins.org.nz/yellow-eyed-penguin-biology.html }} Its eye is yellow. The foreneck and sides of the head are light brown. The back and tail are slate blue-black. Its chest, stomach, thighs and the underside of its flippers are white in colour. Juvenile birds have a greyer head with no yellow band around their eyes. It is the largest living penguin to breed on the mainland of New Zealand and the fourth or fifth heaviest living penguin by body mass.{{cite book |title=''CRC Handbook of Avian Body Masses'' |edition=2nd |editor-first=John B. Jr. |editor-last=Dunning |publisher=CRC Press |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-4200-6444-5}} It stands {{convert|62-79|cm|in|||abbr=}} tall and weighs {{Convert|3-8.5|kg|lb|abbr=}}.Marion, Remi (1999). ''Penguins: A Worldwide Guide''. Sterling Publishing Co. {{ISBN|0-8069-4232-0}} Weight varies throughout the year, with penguins being heaviest just before moulting, during which they may lose 3–4 kilograms in weight.{{Cite web|url=https://www.yellow-eyedpenguin.org.nz/penguins/about-the-yellow-eyed-penguin/moulting/ |title=Moulting|website=Yellow-eyed Penguin Trust|access-date=2019-09-29|archive-date=29 September 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190929012957/https://www.yellow-eyedpenguin.org.nz/penguins/about-the-yellow-eyed-penguin/moulting/ |url-status=live}} Males at around {{convert|5.53|kg|lb|abbr=on}} on average are somewhat heavier than females at an average of {{convert|5.13|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.{{Cite web |url=http://www.dmns.org/media/376641/pseries2,v10red3(82-150).pdf |title=Introduced Mammals |access-date=3 July 2012 |archive-date=22 March 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160322150539/http://www.dmns.org/media/376641/pseries2,v10red3(82-150).pdf }} The yellow-eyed penguin may be long lived, with some individuals reaching 20 years of age. Males are generally longer lived than females, leading to a sex ratio of 2:1 around the age of 10–12 years.{{cite book|title=A Population Study of Penguins |last=Richdale|first=L. |publisher=Oxford University Press |year=1957|location=Oxford}} The yellow-eyed penguin is mostly silent. It makes a shrill bray-like call at nest and breeding sites. ==Distribution and habitat== [[File:Yellow-eyed Penguin, Catlins, New Zealand.JPG|thumb|left|Juvenile in [[The Catlins]], New Zealand]] Until recently, it was assumed that ''M. a. antipodes'' was widespread and abundant before the arrival of Polynesian settlers in New Zealand. However, genetic analysis has since revealed that its [[range (biology)|range]] only expanded to include mainland New Zealand in the past 200 years. Yellow-eyed penguins expanded out of the [[subantarctic]] to replace New Zealand's [[Endemism|endemic]] [[Waitaha penguin]] (''M. a. waitaha''). The Waitaha penguin became extinct between about 1300 and 1500, soon after [[Immigration to New Zealand|Polynesian settlers arrived in New Zealand]].{{Cite web |title=Sentinels of change: prehistoric penguin species raise conservation conundrum |url=https://sciblogs.co.nz/lost-worlds/2018/11/27/sentinels-of-change-prehistoric-penguin-species-raise-conservation-conundrum/ |access-date=2019-09-29 |website=Sciblogs |archive-date=1 October 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191001155953/https://sciblogs.co.nz/lost-worlds/2018/11/27/sentinels-of-change-prehistoric-penguin-species-raise-conservation-conundrum/ |url-status=live }} Jeremy Austin, a member of the team that discovered the Waitaha penguin, said, "Our findings demonstrate that yellow-eyed penguins on mainland New Zealand are not a declining remnant of a previous abundant population, but came from the subantarctic relatively recently and replaced the extinct Waitaha penguin."{{Cite web |title=New penguin species found in New Zealand |url=https://www.adelaide.edu.au/news/print30583.html |access-date=2022-04-22 |website=www.adelaide.edu.au |language=en |archive-date=9 October 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161009120506/https://www.adelaide.edu.au/news/print30583.html |url-status=live }} An [[Insular dwarfism|island dwarf]] subspecies from the Chatham Islands, ''M. a. richdalei,'' is extinct. The modern population of yellow-eyed penguins does not breed on the Chatham Islands. Today, yellow-eyed penguins are found in two distinct populations.{{Cite web|url=https://www.yellow-eyedpenguin.org.nz/penguins/distribution-and-habitat/|title=Distribution and habitat|website=Yellow-eyed Penguin Trust|access-date=2019-09-29|archive-date=29 September 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190929012955/https://www.yellow-eyedpenguin.org.nz/penguins/distribution-and-habitat/|url-status=live}} The northern population extends along the southeast coast of the South Island of New Zealand, down to [[Stewart Island]] / Rakiura and [[Codfish Island]] / Whenua Hou. It includes four main breeding areas in [[Banks Peninsula]], [[North Otago]], Otago Peninsula and the [[The Catlins|Catlins]]. It is also referred to as the mainland population. The southern population includes the subantarctic Auckland Islands and [[Campbell Island / Motu Ihupuku]]. There is little [[gene flow]] between the northern and southern populations as the large stretch of ocean between the South Island and subantarctic region and the subtropical convergence act as a natural barrier.{{Cite journal |last1=Boessenkool |first1=Sanne|last2=Star|first2=Bastiaan|last3=Waters|first3=Jonathan M.|last4=Seddon|first4=Philip J.|date=June 2009 |title=Multilocus assignment analyses reveal multiple units and rare migration events in the recently expanded yellow-eyed penguin (Megadyptes antipodes) |journal=Molecular Ecology|volume=18|issue=11|pages=2390–2400|doi=10.1111/j.1365-294X.2009.04203.x|pmid=19457203|bibcode=2009MolEc..18.2390B |s2cid=205361804}} Based on monitoring between 2012-2017, there are on average 577 breeding pairs per year on Enderby Island in the Auckland Islands, which comprise 37-49% of the total breeding population for the species.{{Cite journal |last1=Muller |first1=Christopher G. |last2=Chilvers |first2=Louise |last3=French |first3=Rebecca K. |last4=Hiscock |first4=Johanna A. |last5=Battley |first5=Phil F. |date=2020-01-01 |title=Population estimate for yellow-eyed penguins (Megadyptes antipodes) in the subantarctic Auckland Islands, New Zealand |url=https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Q124159896 |journal=Notornis |language=English |volume=67 |issue=1 |pages=299–319 |doi=10.63172/057021puhwtc |access-date=7 January 2024 |archive-date=7 May 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240507054527/https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Q124159896 |url-status=live }} == Behaviour == === Breeding === [[File:Megadyptes antipodes -Otago Peninsula, Dunedin, New Zealand -family-8.jpg|thumbnail|A family of yellow-eyed penguin at the Penguin Place Lodge at [[Otago Peninsula]], [[Dunedin]], New Zealand]] The nesting behaviour of yellow-eyed penguins is thought to be the least [[Bird colony|colonial]] of all penguin species. While other penguin species nest in large aggregations, yellow-eyed penguins tend to nest in solitary pairs or loose aggregations. While they can be seen coming ashore in groups of four to six or more individuals, they then disperse along tracks to individual nest sites up to one kilometre inland.{{Cite web|last=Grzelewski|first=Derek|date=Jan–Feb 2004|title=Hoiho—still on the brink|url=https://www.nzgeo.com/stories/hoiho-still-on-the-brink/|access-date=2021-07-17|website=New Zealand Geographic|language=en-NZ|archive-date=17 July 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210717045232/https://www.nzgeo.com/stories/hoiho-still-on-the-brink/|url-status=live}}{{Cite journal|last=Darby|first=John T|date=17 April 2003|title=The yellow-eyed penguin (Megadyptes antipodes) on Stewart and Codfish Islands|url=https://www.notornis.osnz.org.nz/system/files/Notornis_50_3_148.pdf|journal=Notornis|volume=50|issue=3 |page=152|doi=10.63172/476000kvnfaa |access-date=22 July 2021|archive-date=24 February 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210224210214/https://www.notornis.osnz.org.nz/system/files/Notornis_50_3_148.pdf|url-status=live}} Accordingly, the consensus among New Zealand penguin workers is to use habitat rather than colony to refer to areas where yellow-eyed penguins nest. The species prefers to nest in forest or scrub habitat with dense vegetation cover.{{Cite journal |last=Clarke |first=Ryan D. |date=2007 |title=The spatial ecology of yellow-eyed penguin nest site selection at breeding areas with different habitat types on the South Island of New Zealand |url=https://ourarchive.otago.ac.nz/esploro/outputs/graduate/The-spatial-ecology-of-yellow-eyed-penguin/9926479050301891 |journal=University of Otago}}{{Cite book |last=Seddon |first=Philip J |url=https://books.google.co.nz/books?hl=en&lr=&id=aX-0CgAAQBAJ&oi=fnd&pg=PA91&dq=yellow+eyed+penguins&ots=1L0HRQ-xPV&sig=ouhvRr7x3fqqze-HGNuOQnY8qqQ&redir_esc=y#v=onepage&q&f=false |title=Penguins: Natural History and Conservation |last2=Ellenberg |first2=Ursula |last3=van Heezik |first3=Yolanda |date=2015-10-05 |publisher=University of Washington Press |isbn=978-0-295-99906-7 |language=en |chapter=Yellow-eyed Penguin (Megadyptes antipodes)}} Adults will incorporate vegetation such as twigs, grasses and leaves into their nests, and often return to the same nesting area each year. Breeding sites are frequented year-round, and adults may continue to defend their nest areas outside of the breeding season. Nests tend to be visually isolated from one another, though they are usually in audible distance from each other.{{Cite journal |last=Darby |first=John T. |last2=Seddon |first2=Phillip J. |date=1990 |title=Breeding Biology of Yellow-Eyed Penguins (Megadyptes antipodes) |url=https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Philip-Seddon/publication/291068149_2_Breeding_Biology_of_Yellow-Eyed_Penguins_Megadyptes_antipodes/links/62252cc797401151d201d541/2-Breeding-Biology-of-Yellow-Eyed-Penguins-Megadyptes-antipodes.pdf |journal=Penguin Biology}}{{Cite journal |last=Seddon |first=Philip J. |last2=Davis |first2=Lloyd S. |date=1989 |title=Nest-Site Selection by Yellow-Eyed Penguins |url=https://academic.oup.com/condor/article/91/3/653-659/5189167 |journal=The Condor |volume=91 |issue=3 |pages=653 |doi=10.2307/1368116}} Yellow-eyed penguins begin forming pairs and breeding at around two years of age.{{Cite book |last=Marchant |first=S |title=Handbook of Australian, New Zealand & Antarctic birds. Ratites to ducks. |last2=Higgins |first2=P |publisher=Oxford University Press |year=1990 |isbn=9780195530681 |location=Melbourne}} Nest site selection takes place from June onwards, with breeding beginning in late August, followed by incubation from September to November. After hatching chicks are guarded by an adult full-time for the first 40–50 days. Incubation duties are shared by both parents, who may spend several days on the nest at a time. For the first six weeks after hatching, the chicks are guarded during the day by one parent while the other is at sea feeding. The foraging adult returns at least daily to feed the chicks and relieve the partner. After the chicks are six weeks of age, both parents go to sea to supply food to their rapidly growing offspring. Chicks usually fledge in mid-February and are totally independent from then on. Chick fledge weights are generally between 5 and 6 kg. === Feeding === [[File:Feeding_dive_of_a_yellow-eyed_penguin.webm|thumbnail|A yellow-eyed penguin diving to the seafloor and catching an [[opalfish]] off the [[Otago Peninsula]]]] Around 90% of the yellow-eyed penguin's diet is made up of fish, chiefly [[Demersal fish|demersal species]] that live near the seafloor, including [[Argentina elongata|silversides]] (''Argentina elongata''), [[blue cod]] (''Parapercis colias''), [[red codling|red cod]] (''Pseudophycis bachus''), and [[opalfish]] (''Hemerocoetes monopterygius'').Moore, P.J.; Wakelin, M.D. 1997: Diet of the yellow-eyed penguin Megadyptes antipodes, South Island, New Zealand, 1991–1993. Marine Ornithology 25:17–29{{cite web|url=https://animaldiversity.org/accounts/Megadyptes_antipodes/|title=Megadyptes antipodes – yellow-eyed penguin|website=[[Animal Diversity Web]]|access-date=2023-09-02|archive-date=20 January 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230120000629/https://animaldiversity.org/accounts/Megadyptes_antipodes/|url-status=live}} Other species taken are [[New Zealand blueback sprat]] (''Sprattus antipodum'') and cephalopods such as arrow squid (''[[Nototodarus sloanii]]''). They also eat some crustaceans, including krill (''[[Nyctiphanes]] australis''). Recently, jellyfish were found to be targeted by the penguins. While initially thought that the birds would prey on jellyfish itself,{{Cite journal|last1=Thiebot|first1=Jean-Baptiste|last2=Arnould|first2=John PY|last3=Gómez-Laich|first3=Agustina|last4=Ito|first4=Kentaro|last5=Kato|first5=Akiko|last6=Mattern|first6=Thomas |last7=Mitamura|first7=Hiromichi|last8=Noda|first8=Takuji|last9=Poupart|first9=Timothée|last10=Quintana|first10=Flavio|last11=Raclot|first11=Thierry|date=2017|title=Jellyfish and other gelata as food for four penguin species – insights from predator-borne videos|journal=Frontiers in Ecology and the Environment|language=en|volume=15|issue=8|pages=437–441|doi=10.1002/fee.1529|bibcode=2017FrEE...15..437T }} deployments of camera loggers revealed that the penguins were going after juvenile fish and fish larvae associated with jellyfish.{{Cite conference |last1=Mattern|first1=Thomas|last2=Ellenberg|first2=Ursula |last3=Heezik|first3=Yolanda Van|last4=Seddon|first4=Philip J |date=2017|title=Penguins hunting jellyfish: main course, side dish or decoration?|language=en|doi=10.13140/RG.2.2.22929.33123}} Breeding penguins usually undertake two kinds of foraging trips: day trips where the birds leave at dawn and return in the evening ranging up to 25 km from their colonies, and shorter evening trips during which the birds are seldom away from their nest longer than four hours or range farther than 7 km.Mattern, T.; Ellenberg, U.; Houston, D.M.; Davis, L.S. 2007: Consistent foraging routes and benthic foraging behaviour in yellow-eyed penguins. Marine Ecology Progress Series 343: 295–306 Yellow-eyed penguins are known to be an almost exclusive [[Benthic zone|benthic]] forager that searches for prey along the seafloor. Accordingly, up to 90% of their dives are benthic dives. This also means that their average dive depths are determined by the water depths within their home ranges,Mattern, T.; Ellenberg, U.; Houston, D.M.; Lamare, M.; van Heezik, Y.; Seddon, P.J., Davis, L.S. 2013: The Pros and Cons of being a benthic forager: How anthropogenic alterations of the seafloor affect Yellow-eyed penguis. Keynote presentation. [https://web.archive.org/web/20131111100029/http://combine.cs.bris.ac.uk/opencms/ipc/materials/IPC8_Abstract_Book_complete.pdf 8th International Penguin Conference, Bristol, UK. 2–6 September 2013] but can swim up to 240 meters below the water surface.{{cite book |last1=Heather |first1=B. D. |last2=Robertson |first2=Hugh A. |title=The field guide to the birds of New Zealand |date=2000 |publisher=Viking |location=Auckland, N.Z |isbn=0-670-89370-6 |page=227 |edition=Rev.}} ==Conservation== [[File:Žutooki pingvini na Novom Zelandu.jpg|thumb|Yellow-eyed penguins on [[Otago Peninsula]]]] The yellow-eyed penguin is considered one of the rarest penguin species in the world.{{Cite web|url=https://www.yellow-eyedpenguin.org.nz/penguins/population-recent-trends/|title=Population and recent trends|website=Yellow-eyed Penguin Trust|access-date=2019-09-29|archive-date=29 September 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190929013239/https://www.yellow-eyedpenguin.org.nz/penguins/population-recent-trends/|url-status=live}} It is listed on the [[IUCN Red List]] as being endangered. It was first included on the list in 1988 when it was listed as threatened, and was changed to endangered in the year 2000.{{Cite web|url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/en|title=The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species|website=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species|access-date=2019-09-29|archive-date=4 March 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200304172225/https://www.iucnredlist.org/en|url-status=live}} === Northern population === Around a third of the total yellow-eyed penguin population live on the mainland. Data from the [[Department of Conservation (New Zealand)|Department of Conservation]] showed that the northern population had collapsed by 80 percent since 2008, from 739 breeding pairs to just 143 in 2025.{{Cite web |last=MacDuff |first=Keiller |date=29 August 2025 |title=Conservation groups want emergency fishing ban to save hoiho |url=https://www.rnz.co.nz/news/national/571402/conservation-groups-want-emergency-fishing-ban-to-save-hoiho |access-date=2025-09-22 |website=RNZ |language=en-nz}} Experts is likely that the group could disappear within the next 20 years if the trend continues.{{Cite news |last=Fox |first=Alex |date=21 November 2024 |title=How Scientists' Tender Loving Care Could Save This Endangered Penguin Species |url=https://www.smithsonianmag.com/science-nature/how-scientists-tender-loving-care-could-save-this-endangered-penguin-species-180985230/ |access-date=10 December 2024 |work=The Smithsonian}} The last breeding-age female at [[Sandfly Bay]] died in 2024, after being caught in a set net, leaving the site functionally extinct.{{Cite web |last=John |first=Mark |date=24 June 2024 |title=Net kills last breeding-age female hoiho |url=https://www.odt.co.nz/news/dunedin/net-kills-last-breeding-age-female-hoiho |access-date=2025-12-05 |website=Otago Daily Times |language=en}}{{Cite web |last=Vance |first=Andrea |date=10 August 2025 |title=She was the last: Penguin starvation crisis sparks call for emergency fishing ban |url=https://www.thepost.co.nz/politics/360780478/she-was-last-penguin-starvation-crisis-sparks-call-emergency-fishing-ban |access-date=2025-12-05 |website=The Post}} === Southern population === The subantarctic population, where the majority of the species breeds, is less studied. Monitoring of the 2015 to 2017 breeding seasons found that fledging success tended to be higher than on the mainland, although chicks were smaller and lighter on average.{{Cite journal |last1=Muller |first1=C. G. |last2=Chilvers |first2=B. L. |last3=French |first3=R. K. |last4=Battley |first4=P. F. |date=2024 |title=Variable breeding success and its implication in the conservation of endangered yellow-eyed penguin (Megadyptes antipodes) at the New Zealand subantarctic Auckland Islands |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1002/aqc.4143 |journal=Aquatic Conservation: Marine and Freshwater Ecosystems |language=en |volume=34 |issue=4 |article-number=e4143 |doi=10.1002/aqc.4143 |bibcode=2024ACMFE..34E4143M |issn=1099-0755}} === Threats === [[File:Yellow-eyed Penguin crying MC.jpg|thumb|upright|Penguin calling at [[Curio Bay]], New Zealand]] Populations of yellow-eyed penguins are threatened by multiple factors. A risk assessment published in November 2025 indicated the biggest threats to the species are malnutrition, predation, disease, other trauma/unidentifiable sources, and fishing.{{Cite web |last=Clark |first=Tom |date=11 March 2026 |title=Hoiho face extinction in the wild, despite our best endeavours |url=https://www.odt.co.nz/opinion/hoiho-face-extinction-wild-despite-our-best-endeavours |access-date=2026-03-13 |website=Otago Daily Times |language=en}} The northern population is declining as a result of recurring poor breeding seasons and ongoing high adult mortality. Threats at sea include poor foraging success, and becoming caught in fisheries equipment.{{Cite journal |last1=Mattern |first1=Thomas |last2=Kerry-Jayne Wilson |date=2018 |title=New Zealand penguins - current knowledge and research priorities |url=http://rgdoi.net/10.13140/RG.2.2.16180.50564/3 |journal=Birds New Zealand |language=en |doi=10.13140/RG.2.2.16180.50564/3}} Chicks are often lost at sea, with only 20% projected to make it back to land for nesting, and only 5% expected to breed. Studies carried out during the 1980s showed that [[Red codling|red cod]] is a key part of their diet, however red cod populations have since declined, and more recent investigation has found that the penguins are feeding on [[blue cod]] instead, which provide less suitable nutrition. Disease also poses a key threat to the species, which poor nutrition makes them more vulnerable to. A previously undescribed disease killed off 60% of yellow-eyed penguin chicks on the [[Otago Peninsula]] and in [[North Otago]] in 2004. The disease has been described as [[diphtheritic stomatitis]], caused by infection of ''[[Corynebacterium]]'', a genus of bacteria that also causes [[diphtheria]] in humans.{{cite journal |last1=Saunderson |first1=SC |last2=Nouioui |first2=I |last3=Midwinter |first3=AC |last4=Wilkinson |first4=DA |last5=Young |first5=MJ |last6=McInnes |first6=KM |last7=Watts |first7=J |last8=Sangal |first8=V |title=Phylogenomic Characterization of a Novel ''Corynebacterium'' Species Associated with Fatal Diphtheritic Stomatitis in Endangered Yellow-Eyed Penguins |journal=mSystems |date=29 June 2021 |volume=6 |issue=3 |pages=e0032021 |doi=10.1128/mSystems.00320-21 |doi-access=free|pmid=34100641 |pmc=8269222 }} A similar problem has affected the Stewart Island population.Kerrie Waterworth, [http://www.stuff.co.nz/4287560a7693.html Mystery illness strikes penguins] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080506001549/http://www.stuff.co.nz/4287560a7693.html |date=6 May 2008 }}, Sunday Star Times, 25 November 2007. In 2023, a novel [[Gyrovirus]] was identified as the likely cause of the mysterious and fatal respiratory illness which had killed roughly a quarter of the yellow-eyed penguin chicks from the mainland during the 2021 November to December hatching season.{{Cite journal |last1=Wierenga |first1=Janelle R. |last2=Morgan |first2=Kerri J. |last3=Hunter |first3=Stuart |last4=Taylor |first4=Harry S. |last5=Argilla |first5=Lisa S. |last6=Webster |first6=Trudi |last7=Dubrulle |first7=Jeremy |last8=Jorge |first8=Fátima |last9=Bostina |first9=Mihnea |last10=Burga |first10=Laura |last11=Holmes |first11=Edward C. |last12=McInnes |first12=Kate |last13=Geoghegan |first13=Jemma L. |date=2023-02-01 |title=A novel gyrovirus is abundant in yellow-eyed penguin (Megadyptes antipodes) chicks with a fatal respiratory disease |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0042682222002239 |journal=Virology |volume=579 |pages=75–83 |doi=10.1016/j.virol.2022.12.012 |pmid=36608597 |issn=0042-6822}} The disease has a mortality rate of more than 90%.{{Cite web |date=19 January 2023 |title=Researchers identify gyrovirus threatening critically endangered penguin chicks |url=https://www.rnz.co.nz/news/national/482687/researchers-identify-gyrovirus-threatening-critically-endangered-penguin-chicks |access-date=2025-09-22 |website=RNZ |language=en-nz}} It was estimated in 2023 around 320 individuals died. === Mitigations === A reserve protecting more than 10% of the mainland population was established at Long Point in [[the Catlins]] in November 2007 by the Department of Conservation and the Yellow-eyed Penguin Trust.Gwyneth Hyndman, [http://www.stuff.co.nz/stuff/southlandtimes/4290682a6568.html Land set aside for yellow-eyed penguin protection in Catlins] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080506011206/http://www.stuff.co.nz/stuff/southlandtimes/4290682a6568.html |date=6 May 2008 }}. The Southland Times, Wednesday, 28 November 2007.[http://www.radionz.co.nz/news/latest/200711271338/12km_coastal_reserve_declared_for_yellow-eyed_penguins 12km coastal reserve declared for yellow-eyed penguins] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120213000040/http://www.radionz.co.nz/news/latest/200711271338/12km_coastal_reserve_declared_for_yellow-eyed_penguins |date=13 February 2012 }}, Radio New Zealand News, 27 November 2007. The species was granted protection under the U.S. [[Endangered Species Act]] in 2010.[http://www.seaturtles.org/article.php?id=1742 Five Penguins Win U.S. Endangered Species Act Protection] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101128032547/http://seaturtles.org/article.php?id=1742 |date=28 November 2010 }} Turtle Island Restoration Network Due to extreme disease outbreak and subsequent mortalities, the [[Dunedin Wildlife Hospital]] began treating chicks with disease symptoms or weight loss. In 2022, the hospital successfully incubated and hatched eggs for the first time.{{Cite web |last=Mulligan |first=Jesse |date=20 December 2022 |title=World first at Dunedin Wildlife Hospital, hatching Hoiho |url=https://www.rnz.co.nz/national/programmes/afternoons/audio/2018871945/world-first-at-dunedin-wildlife-hospital-hatching-hoiho |access-date=2025-09-22 |website=RNZ |language=en-nz}} The following year, the hospital hand-reared 214 chicks, as it was estimated that 50 to 70% of chicks would have died without intervention. In September 2025, a three-month emergency ban on set net fishing was put in place around Otago Peninsula to protect the yellow-eyed penguin.{{Cite web |last=MacDuff |first=Keiller |date=15 September 2019 |title=Emergency set net fishing ban enforced to protect threatened hoiho around Otago Peninsula |url=https://www.rnz.co.nz/news/national/573069/emergency-set-net-fishing-ban-enforced-to-protect-threatened-hoiho-around-otago-peninsula |access-date=2025-09-22 |website=RNZ |language=en-nz}} Later that year the ban was extended by another nine months.{{Cite web |date=10 December 2025 |title=Emergency set net fishing ban to protect threatened hoiho around Otago Peninsula extended |url=https://www.rnz.co.nz/news/national/581392/emergency-set-net-fishing-ban-to-protect-threatened-hoiho-around-otago-peninsula-extended |access-date=2025-12-16 |website=RNZ |language=en-nz}} The closure extended the existing four nautical mile set net ban, which was in place to protect dolphins, out to eight nautical miles.{{Cite web |last=James |first=Nick |date=2 December 2025 |title=Lawyers criticise ministry's advice to Fisheries Minister Shane Jones on set net fishing |url=https://www.rnz.co.nz/news/national/580570/lawyers-criticise-ministry-s-advice-to-fisheries-minister-shane-jones-on-set-net-fishing |access-date=2025-12-05 |website=RNZ |language=en-nz}} The ban came after urgent calls from conservation groups as the species entered their breeding season. The following month, the [[Environmental Law Initiative|Environmental Law Initiative (ELI)]] filed High Court proceedings against the [[Shane Jones|Minister for Oceans and Fisheries]] on the basis that the set net ban failed to protect the species against extinction.{{Cite web |date=16 October 2025 |title=Charity takes legal action over set net fishing ban, says hoiho need more protection |url=https://www.rnz.co.nz/news/national/576054/charity-takes-legal-action-over-set-net-fishing-ban-says-hoiho-need-more-protection |access-date=2025-12-05 |website=RNZ |language=en-nz}} The extension applied to waters surrounding the Otago Peninsula, leaving habitats including North Otago and Stewart Island unprotected. In February 2026, the proceedings filed by ELI against the Minister were dismissed by the High Court.{{Cite web |date=4 February 2026 |title=Emergency set net fishing ban to protect threatened hoiho lawful, court rules |url=https://www.rnz.co.nz/news/national/585965/emergency-set-net-fishing-ban-to-protect-threatened-hoiho-lawful-court-rules |access-date=2026-02-04 |website=RNZ |language=en-nz}} In the same ruling, the court recognised that the temporary emergency closure is lawful, and that ministers must work to safeguard the species’ survival.{{Cite news |last=Tukaki |first=Matthew |date=4 February 2026 |title=#nature: High Court Says Minister Must Act to Protect Endangered Hoiho |url=https://waateanews.com/2026/02/04/nature-high-court-says-minister-must-act-to-protect-endangered-hoiho/ |archive-url=http://web.archive.org/web/20260204073430/https://waateanews.com/2026/02/04/nature-high-court-says-minister-must-act-to-protect-endangered-hoiho/ |archive-date=2026-02-04 |access-date=2026-02-05 |work=Waatea News |language=en-NZ}} ==Tourism== Several mainland habitats have [[Bird hide|hides]] and are relatively accessible for those wishing to watch the birds come ashore. These include beaches at [[Oamaru]], the [[Moeraki]] lighthouse, a number of beaches near [[Dunedin]], and the Catlins. In addition, commercial tourist operations on Otago Peninsula also provide hides to view yellow-eyed penguins. However, the yellow-eyed penguin cannot be found in zoos because it will not reproduce in captivity.{{Cite web|url=https://penguin-pedia.com/penguin-species/yellow-eyed-penguins/|title=Yellow-eyed Penguins|website=Penguin Pedia|access-date=2019-07-08|archive-date=3 July 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190703213136/https://penguin-pedia.com/penguin-species/yellow-eyed-penguins/}} Studies have shown however, that human presence in their habitats negatively impacts their foraging and breeding habits.{{Cite journal |last=French |first=Rebecca K. |date=23 October 2018 |title=Behavioural consequences of human disturbance on subantarctic Yellow-eyed Penguins Megadyptes antipodes |url=https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/bird-conservation-international/article/behavioural-consequences-of-human-disturbance-on-subantarctic-yelloweyed-penguins-megadyptes-antipodes/F84A93FEB12B297B02C9226C2FBF4B91 |journal=Bird Conservation International |volume=29 |issue=2 |pages=227–290 |via=Cambridge University}} == In culture == *The hoiho appears on the reverse side of the [[New Zealand five-dollar zh.extract:
黃眼企鵝（學名：Megadyptes antipodes，毛利語：Hoiho）是原住新西蘭的企鵝。牠們以往被認為是小藍企鵝的近親，但分子分析研究發現牠們更為接近冠企鵝屬。就像所有其他的企鵝，黃眼企鵝主要以魚為主食。
黃眼企鵝分佈在新西蘭的南島、斯圖爾特島、奧克蘭群島及坎貝爾島。位於奧塔哥半島的群落是著名的旅遊熱點，很多遊客會在此近距離觀賞黃眼企鵝。


科學分類
黃眼企鵝是黃眼企鵝屬下仅有的2个物種之一。牠們以往被認為是小藍企鵝的近親，但分子分析研究發現牠們更為接近冠企鵝屬。粒線體及細胞核DNA證明牠們是約於1500萬年前從冠企鵝屬的祖先中分化出來。該物種有一個已滅絕的亞種：怀塔哈企鹅。


描述
黃眼企鵝是較為大的企鵝，平均長70公分及重約6.23公斤。牠們的頭部呈淡黃色，瞳孔呈更淡的黃色，身體呈黑色。牠們的顎及喉嚨呈黑褐色。眼睛之間有一黃帶在頭後部連接。雛鳥的頭部較灰，並沒有黃帶，而瞳孔亦是呈灰色的。
黃眼企鵝可以很長壽，有些可達20歲。雄鳥一般較雌鳥長壽，介乎10—12歲的年齡中，雄鳥數目就是雌鳥的兩倍。


分佈及棲息地
黃眼企鵝一般在森林、斜坡或岸邊築巢，巢穴的地方往往都是在海灣的地方。巢穴都是分佈在新西蘭，在南島的東南岸、弗佛海峽、斯圖爾特島、奧克蘭群島及坎貝爾島。


保育
黃眼企鵝是瀕危物種，估計只有約4000隻。牠們是世界上最稀有的企鵝之一。其主要的威脅是失去棲息地、掠食者的出現及環境變遷。牠們是所有現存企鵝中最古老的。
於2007年11月，在卡特林斯海岸成立了一個保育區域，以保護超過10%的黃眼企鵝數量。


健康
在奧塔哥半島及北奧塔哥的黃眼企鵝，於2004年春天感染了不知名的疾病，死去了接近60%的數量。這種疾病與棒狀桿菌屬的感染有關，但是這似乎只是繼發感染，其真正的病原仍未清楚。同樣的問題亦在斯圖爾特島出現。


行為
黃眼企鵝一般會在離岸7—13公里及巢穴以外的17公里處覓食。牠們會在清晨離開巢穴，花2—3日的時間在海中覓食，若要哺育雛鳥的話，則會在即日的晚上回到巢穴。牠們一般可以潛至34米的水深處。


食性
黃眼企鵝會潛至20—60米深的地方覓食。黃眼企鵝約90%的食物都是魚類，餘下的是頭足綱。牠們所吃的魚類有新西蘭擬鱸、紅擬褐鱈、單鰭雙犁魚及藍背黍鯡，長度介乎2—32公分。頭足綱約佔雛鳥飲食的49%。


繁殖

黃眼企鵝是否會佔有領地而一直備受爭議。大部份南極洲的企鵝都會成大群的一同生活，如皇帝企鵝。相反，黃眼企鵝之間不會在視線範圍內築巢。牠們可能會成群4—6隻企鵝一同出海，但卻會各自分散的回到自己的巢穴。現時學者一般都稱黃眼企鵝的巢穴為其棲息地而非領地。


旅遊
在新西蘭的黃眼企鵝棲息地有很多隱蔽地方，可以在岸邊觀賞牠們的生態。這些地方包括有奧瑪魯的海灘、摩拉基燈塔、一些近但尼丁的海灘及卡特林斯海岸。在奧塔哥半島亦有很多商業旅遊的活動來觀賞黃眼企鵝。
